{{Short description|Illegal behavior by minors}}
{{Redirect|Teenage crime|the song|Teenage Crime (song)}}
{{Redirect|Young offenders|the film and TV show|The Young Offenders (disambiguation)}}
{{Globalize|article|the United States|date=June 2020}}
{{Criminology and penology |crimes}}
{{Youth rights sidebar}}
{{Use dmy dates|date=June 2024}}

'''Juvenile delinquency''', also known as '''juvenile offending''', is the act of participating in unlawful behavior younger than the [[statutory]] [[age of majority]].<ref>{{cite book |last1=Siegel |first1=Larry J. |first2=Brandon |last2=Welsh |title=Juvenile Delinquency: The Core |edition= 4th |location=Belmont, CA |publisher=Wadsworth/cengage Learning |year=2011 |isbn=978-0534519322}}</ref> These acts would be considered crimes if the individuals committing them were older.<ref>{{Cite book |last=Blundell |first=Jonathan |title=Cambridge IGCSE Sociology coursebook |publisher=Cambridge University Press |year=2014 |isbn=978-1-107-64513-4 |pages=198}}</ref> The term '''delinquent''' usually refers to juvenile delinquency, and is also generalised to refer to a young person who behaves an unacceptable way.<ref>{{cite web |title=delinquent |website=Cambridge Dictionary |date=26 July 2023 |url=https://dictionary.cambridge.org/dictionary/english/delinquent |access-date=28 July 2023 |quote=a person, usually young, who behaves in a way that is illegal or not acceptable to most people}}</ref>

In the United States, a juvenile delinquent is a person who commits a [[crime]] and is under a specific age.<ref>{{Cite web |date=19 February 2015 |title=38. 'Juvenile' Defined |url=https://www.justice.gov/archives/jm/criminal-resource-manual-38-juvenile-defined |access-date=30 April 2022 |publisher=US Department of Justice |language=en}}</ref> Most states specify a juvenile delinquent, or '''young offender''', as an individual under 18 years of age, while a few states have set the maximum age slightly different. The term "juvenile delinquent" originated from the late 18th and early 19th centuries when the treatment of juvenile and adult criminals was similar, and punishment was over the seriousness of an offense.<ref>{{cite web|url=https://www.ojp.gov/ncjrs/virtual-library/abstracts/innocence-and-experience-evolution-concept-juvenile-delinquency-mid|title=Innocence and Experience - The Evolution of the Concept of Juvenile Deliquency in the Mid-Nineteenth Century|publisher=US Department of Justice|access-date=2 October 2023}}</ref> Before the 18th century, juveniles over age 7 were tried in the same criminal court as adults and, if convicted, could get the death penalty. Illinois established the first juvenile court. This juvenile court focused on treatment objectives instead of punishment, determined appropriate terminology associated with juvenile offenders, and made juvenile records confidential. In 2021, Michigan, New York, and Vermont raised the maximum age to under 19, and Vermont law was updated again in 2022 to include individuals under 20.<ref name="nga20210812">{{Cite web |date=12 August 2021 |title=Age Boundaries in Juvenile Justice Systems |url=https://www.nga.org/center/publications/age-boundaries-in-juvenile-justice-systems/ |access-date=30 April 2022 |publisher=National Governors Association}}</ref> Only three states, Georgia, Texas, and Wisconsin, still appropriate the age of a juvenile delinquent as someone under the age of 17.<ref>{{Cite web |title=Juvenile Age of Jurisdiction and Transfer to Adult Court Laws |url=https://www.ncsl.org/research/civil-and-criminal-justice/juvenile-age-of-jurisdiction-and-transfer-to-adult-court-laws.aspx |access-date=30 April 2022 |website=www.ncsl.org}}</ref> While the maximum age in some US states has increased, Japan has lowered the juvenile delinquent age from under 20 to under 18. This change occurred on 1 April 2022 when the [[National Diet|Japanese Diet]] activated a law lowering the age of minor status in the country.<ref>{{Cite web |title=Revised Civil Code and Juvenile Law in April to lower legal age of adulthood to 18 |url=https://japantoday.com/category/national/revised-civil-code-and-juvenile-law-in-april-to-lower-legal-age-of-adulthood-to-18 |access-date=30 April 2022 |website=Japan Today |date=4 January 2022 |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web |date=21 May 2021 |title=Japan's Juvenile Law Amended for Tougher Penalties |url=https://www.nippon.com/en/news/yjj2021052100218/ |access-date=30 April 2022 |website=nippon.com |language=en |archive-date=9 August 2022 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220809065329/https://www.nippon.com/en/news/yjj2021052100218/ |url-status=dead }}</ref><ref>{{Cite news |date=22 May 2021 |title=Editorial: Concerns remain over amendment damping Japan juvenile law's rehab principles |language=en |work=Mainichi Daily News |url=https://mainichi.jp/english/articles/20210522/p2a/00m/0na/018000c |access-date=30 April 2022}}</ref> Just as there are differences in the maximum age of a juvenile delinquent, the minimum age for a child to be considered capable of delinquency or the [[age of criminal responsibility]] varies considerably between the states.<ref name="nga20210812" /> Some states that impose a minimum age have made recent amendments to raise the minimum age. Still, most states remain ambiguous on the minimum age for a child to be determined a juvenile delinquent. In 2021, North Carolina changed the minimum age from 6 to 10 years old, Connecticut moved from 7 to 10, and New York adjusted from 7 to 12. In some states, the minimum age depends on the seriousness of the crime committed.<ref>{{Cite web |title=Juvenile Justice 2021 Year-End Report |url=https://www.ncsl.org/research/civil-and-criminal-justice/juvenile-justice-2021-year-end-report.aspx |access-date=30 April 2022 |website=ncsl.org}}</ref> Juvenile delinquents or [[Juvenile offender|juvenile offenders]] commit crimes ranging from [[Status offense|status offenses]] such as, truancy, violating a curfew or underage drinking and smoking to more serious offenses categorized as [[Property crime|property crimes]], [[Violent crime|violent crimes]], [[sexual offenses]], and [[Cybercrime|cybercrimes]].<ref>{{Cite web |title=Juvenile Crimes – Juvenile Law Statistics – Impact Law |url=https://www.impactlaw.com/criminal-law/juvenile/crimes |access-date=30 April 2022 |website=impactlaw.com |language=en}}</ref>

Some scholars have found an increase in youth arrests and have concluded that this may reflect more aggressive criminal justice and [[zero-tolerance]] policies rather than changes in youth behavior.<ref name="NewYorkTimes">{{cite news |last=Goode |first=Erica |date=19 December 2011 |title=Since 2008, "an estimated 60% of children in the United States were exposed to violence, crime, or abuse in their homes, schools, and communities within the past year. Approximately 46% were assaulted at least once in the past year and 10% were injured in an assault."(crimesolutions.gov)The violence in the households of teenagers can/will have a significant impact in the lives of the teenagers as they grow up. Many in U.S. Are Arrested by Age 23, Study Finds |newspaper=The New York Times |url=https://www.nytimes.com/2011/12/19/us/nearly-a-third-of-americans-are-arrested-by-23-study-says.html |access-date=3 November 2014 |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20140712185722/http://www.nytimes.com/2011/12/19/us/nearly-a-third-of-americans-are-arrested-by-23-study-says.html |archive-date=12 July 2014 }}</ref> Youth violence rates in the United States have dropped to approximately 12% of peak rates in 1993, according to official U.S. government statistics, suggesting that most juvenile offending is non-violent.<ref>{{cite web|url=http://www.childstats.gov/americaschildren/beh5.asp|title=Childstats.gov – America's Young Adults: Special Issue, 2014 – Contraception|work=childstats.gov|url-status=live|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20140626222458/http://www.childstats.gov/americaschildren/beh5.asp|archive-date=26 June 2014}}</ref> Many delinquent acts can be attributed to the environmental factors such as family behavior or peer influence.<ref name="Graham4" /><ref name="Bartol2" /> One contributing factor that has gained attention in recent years is the [[school-to-prison pipeline]]. According to Diverse Education, nearly 75% of states have built more jails and prisons than colleges. CNN also provides a diagram that shows that the cost per inmate is significantly higher in most states than the cost per student. This shows that taxpayers' dollars are going toward providing for prisoners rather than providing for the educational system and promoting the advancement of education. For every school built, the focus on punitive punishment has correlated with juvenile delinquency rates.<ref name="Theriot2009" /> Some have suggested shifting from [[zero tolerance|zero-tolerance]] policies to [[restorative justice]] approaches.<ref name="Fowler2010" />

[[Youth detention center|Juvenile detention centers]], [[juvenile court]]s, and [[Electronic monitoring in the United States|electronic monitoring]] are common structures of the juvenile legal system.<ref name="youth2019" /> Juvenile courts are in place to address offenses as civil rather than criminal cases in most instances. The frequency of use and structure of these courts in the United States varies by state.<ref>{{Cite book|url=https://www.nap.edu/read/9747/chapter/7|title=Read "Juvenile Crime, Juvenile Justice" at NAP.edu|year=2001|doi=10.17226/9747|isbn=978-0-309-06842-0|language=en}}</ref> Depending on the type and severity of the offense committed, individuals under 18 may be charged and treated as adults.<ref name="eji" />

==Overview==
Juvenile delinquency, or offending, is often separated into three categories:
* delinquency, crimes committed by [[Minor (law)|minors]] younger than the [[statutory]] [[age of majority]], which are dealt with by the [[juvenile court]]s and justice system;
* criminal behavior, [[crime]]s dealt with by the [[criminal justice system]];
*[[status offense]]s, offenses that are only classified as such because only a minor can commit them. One example of this is possession of alcohol by a minor. These offenses are also dealt with by the juvenile courts.<ref>{{cite book |last1=Woolard |last2=Scott |year=2009 |chapter=The legal regulation of adolescence |editor-first=R. |editor-last=Lerner |editor2-first=L. |editor2-last=Steinberg |title=Handbook of Adolescent psychology |edition= 3rd |volume=2 |pages=345–371 |location=New York |publisher=Wiley |isbn=9780470149225 }}</ref>

Currently, there is not an agency whose jurisdiction is tracking worldwide juvenile delinquency, but [[UNICEF]] estimates that over one million children are in some detention globally.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.unicef.org/protection/files/Justice_for_Children_Detention.pdf|title=JUSTICE FOR CHILDREN: DETENTION AS A LAST RESORT Innovative Initiatives in the East Asia and Pacific Region|website=United Nations Children's Fund}}</ref> Many countries do not keep records of the amount of delinquent or detained juveniles, but of the ones that do, the United States has the highest number of juvenile delinquency cases.<ref>{{Cite book|chapter-url=https://www.hrw.org/world-report/2016/children-behind-bars|title=World Report 2016: Rights Trends in Children Behind Bars|chapter=Children Behind Bars: The Global Overuse of Detention of Children |date=22 December 2015|language=en|access-date=4 March 2020}}</ref> In the United States, the [[Office of Juvenile Justice and Delinquency Prevention]] compiles data concerning trends in juvenile delinquency. According to their most recent publication, 7 in 1,000 juveniles in the U.S. committed a serious crime in 2016.<ref name="qa03201">{{Cite web|url=https://www.ojjdp.gov/ojstatbb/offenders/qa03201.asp?qaDate=2016|title=Victimizations by juveniles per 1,000 youth ages 12-17, 1980–2016|last=Office of Juvenile Justice and Delinquency Prevention|first=US Department of Justice|date=22 October 2018|website=www.ojjdp.gov|access-date=4 March 2020}}</ref> The U.S. Department of Justice defines a serious crime as one of the following eight offenses: murder and non-negligent homicide, rape (legacy & revised), robbery, aggravated assault, burglary, motor vehicle theft, larceny theft, and arson.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.ucrdatatool.gov/offenses.cfm|title=Uniform Crime Reporting Statistics|last=Uniform Crime Reporting Statistics|first=Federal Bureau of Investigation|website=ucrdatatool.gov|access-date=4 March 2020}}</ref> According to research compiled by James Howell in 2009, the juvenile arrest rate has been dropping consistently since its peak in 1994.<ref>{{Cite book|last=Howell|first=James|url=http://sk.sagepub.com/books/preventing-and-reducing-juvenile-delinquency-2e|title=Preventing and Reducing Juvenile Delinquency: A Comprehensive Framework|date=2009|publisher=SAGE Publications, Inc.|isbn=978-1-4129-5638-3|location=Thousand Oaks, California|doi=10.4135/9781452274980}}</ref> Of the cases for juvenile delinquency that make it through the court system, probation is the most common consequence, and males account for over 70% of the caseloads.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://ojjdp.ojp.gov/sites/g/files/xyckuh176/files/media/document/datasnapshot_jcs2015.pdf|title=Characteristics of delinquency cases handled in juvenile court in 2015|last=OJJDP National Juvenile Court Data|first=Office of Juvenile Justice and Delinquency Prevention|date=2015}}</ref><ref name="qa03201" />

According to developmental research by Moffitt (2006),<ref name="SteinbergAdolescence2">{{cite book|last=Steinberg|first=L.|title=Adolescence|publisher=McGraw-Hill|year=2008|isbn=9780073405483|edition=8th|location=New York}}</ref> two types of offenders emerge in adolescence. The first is an age-specific offender, referred to as the adolescence-limited offender, for whom juvenile offending or delinquency begins and ends during their period of [[adolescence]]. Moffitt argues that most teenagers tend to show some form of [[Anti-social behaviour|antisocial]] or delinquent behavior during adolescence; it is therefore essential to account for these behaviors in childhood to determine whether they will be adolescence-limited offenders or something more long-term.<ref name="Moffitt2">{{cite book|last=Moffitt|title=Developmental Psychopathy|publisher=Wiley|year=2006|isbn=9781315094908|editor1-last=Cicchetti|editor1-first=D.|edition=2nd|location=New York|pages=75–103|chapter=Life course persistent versus adolescent limited antisocial behavior|doi=10.4324/9781315094908-4|editor2-last=Cohen|editor2-first=D.}}</ref> The other type of offender is the repeat offender, referred to as the life-course-persistent offender, who begins offending or showing antisocial/aggressive behavior in adolescence (or even in [[childhood]]) and continues into [[adult]]hood.<ref name="Moffitt2"/>

==Situational factors==
Most of the influencing factors for juvenile delinquency tend to be caused by a mix of both genetic and environmental factors.<ref name="SteinbergAdolescence2"/> According to Laurence Steinberg's book ''Adolescence'', the two most significant predictors of juvenile delinquency are [[Parenting styles|parenting style]] and [[Peer group|peer group association]].<ref name="SteinbergAdolescence2" /> Additional factors that may lead a teenager into juvenile delinquency include poor or low [[socioeconomic status]], poor school readiness/performance, and/or failure and [[peer rejection]]. Delinquent activity, especially the involvement in [[Youth Gangs|youth gangs]], may also be caused by a desire for protection against violence or financial hardship. Juvenile offenders can view delinquent activity as a means of gaining access to resources to protect against such threats. Research by Carrie Dabb indicates that even changes in the weather can increase the likelihood of children exhibiting deviant behavior.<ref>{{cite book|last1=Dabb|first1=C|url=https://digitalcommons.usu.edu/cgi/viewcontent.cgi?article=3651&context=etd|title=The relationship between weather and children's behavior: a study of teacher perceptions|date=May 1997|publisher=USU Thesis}}</ref>

==Family environment==
:Family factors that may have an influence on offending include: the level of [[parental supervision]], the way parents [[discipline]] a child, parental conflict or [[Legal separation|separation]], criminal activity by parents or siblings, [[Child abuse|parental abuse or neglect]], and the quality of the parent-child relationship.<ref name="Graham4">Graham, J. & Bowling, B. (1995). Young People and Crime, Home Office Research Study No. 145, London: Home Office.</ref> As mentioned above, [[Parenting styles|parenting style]] is not of the largest predictors of juvenile delinquency. There are 4 categories of parenting styles which describe the attitudes and behaviors that parents express while raising their children.<ref name="Bi-Yang-Li-Wang-Zhang-Deater-Deckard-2018">{{Cite journal|last1=Bi|first1=Xinwen|last2=Yang|first2=Yiqun|last3=Li|first3=Hailei|last4=Wang|first4=Meiping|last5=Zhang|first5=Wenxin|last6=Deater-Deckard|first6=Kirby|date=2018|title=Parenting Styles and Parent–Adolescent Relationships: The Mediating Roles of Behavioral Autonomy and Parental Authority|journal=Frontiers in Psychology|language=en|volume=9|article-number=2187|doi=10.3389/fpsyg.2018.02187|pmid=30483194|pmc=6243060|issn=1664-1078|doi-access=free}}</ref>
:* Authoritative parenting is characterized by warmth and support in addition to discipline.
:* Indulgent parenting is characterized by warmth and regard towards their children but lack structure and discipline.
:*Authoritarian parenting is characterized by high discipline without the warmth thus leading to often hostile demeanor and harsh correction
:*Neglectful parenting is both non responsive and non demanding. The child is not engaged either affectionately or disciplinary by the parent.<ref name="Bi-Yang-Li-Wang-Zhang-Deater-Deckard-2018" />

According to research done by Laura E. Berk, the style of parenting that would be most beneficial for a child, based on studies conducted by Diana Baumrind (1971) is the authoritative child-rearing style because it combines acceptance with discipline to render healthy development for the child.<ref>{{Cite journal|last=Baumrind|first=Diana|date=1971|title=Current patterns of parental authority.|journal=Developmental Psychology|volume=4|issue=1, Pt.2|pages=1–103|doi=10.1037/h0030372|issn=1939-0599|hdl=10818/16710|hdl-access=free}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book|last=Berk, Laura E.|title=Development through the lifespan|year=2014|isbn=9780205957606|edition=Sixth|location=Boston|oclc=830646471}}</ref>

As concluded in Steinberg's ''Adolescence'', children brought up by single parents are more likely to live in poverty and engage in delinquent behavior than those who live with both parents.<ref name="SteinbergAdolescence2" /> However, according to research done by Graham and Bowling, once the attachment a child feels towards their parent(s) and the level of parental supervision are taken into account, children in single parent families are no more likely to offend than others. It was seen that when a child has low parental supervision they are much more likely to offend.<ref name="Graham4" /><ref name="Farrington2">{{cite book|last=Farrington|first=D. P.|title=The Oxford Handbook of Criminology|publisher=Oxford University Press|year=2002|isbn=978-0199256099|editor1-last=Maguire|editor1-first=M.|edition=3rd|location=Oxford|chapter=Developmental criminology and risk-focused prevention|display-editors=etal}}</ref> Negative [[Peer group|peer group association]] is more likely when adolescents are left unsupervised.<ref name="SteinbergAdolescence2"/> A lack of supervision is also connected to poor relationships between children and parents. Children who are often in conflict with their parents may be less willing to discuss their activities with them.<ref name="Graham4" /> Conflict between a child's parents is also much more closely linked to offending than being raised by a lone parent.<ref name="Walklate2">Walklate, S (2003). ''Understanding Criminology – Current Theoretical Debates'', 2nd edition, Maidenhead: Open University Press.</ref>

Adolescents with [[sibling]]s who have committed crimes are more likely to be influenced by their siblings and become delinquent if the sibling is older, of the same sex/gender, and maintains a good relationship with the child.<ref name="Bartol2">Bartol, Curt & Bartol, Anne (2009). ''Juvenile Delinquency and Antisocial Behavior: A Developmental Perspective'', 3rd ed. Upper Saddle River, NJ: Pearson Prentice Hall.</ref> Cases where a younger criminal sibling influences an older one are rare. An aggressive more hostile sibling is less likely to influence a younger sibling in the direction of delinquency, if anything, the more strained the relationship between the siblings, the less they will want to be an influence on each other.<ref name="Bartol2" />

Children resulting from [[unintended pregnancies]] are more likely to exhibit delinquent behavior.<ref name="Monea2011">{{cite journal|vauthors=Monea J, Thomas A|date=June 2011|title=Unintended pregnancy and taxpayer spending|journal=Perspectives on Sexual and Reproductive Health|volume=43|issue=2|pages=88–93|doi=10.1363/4308811|pmid=21651707}}</ref> They also have lower mother-child relationship quality.<ref name="HP2020FP">{{Unbulleted list citebundle
|1={{cite web|url=http://healthypeople.gov/2020/topicsobjectives2020/overview.aspx?topicid=13|title=Family Planning – Healthy People 2020|url-status=live|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20101228012908/http://www.healthypeople.gov/2020/topicsobjectives2020/overview.aspx?topicid=13|archive-date=28 December 2010|access-date=18 August 2011}}
|2={{cite web|url=http://www.childtrends.org/Files/Child_Trends-2007_05_01_FR_Consequences.pdf|title=The consequences of unintended childbearing: A white paper|date=May 2007|website=Child Trends|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20100702204001/http://www.childtrends.org/Files/Child_Trends-2007_05_01_FR_Consequences.pdf|archive-date=2 July 2010|vauthors=Logan C, Holcombe E, Manlove J, etal}}
|3={{cite journal|vauthors=Cheng D, Schwarz E, Douglas E, etal|date=March 2009|title=Unintended pregnancy and associated maternal preconception, prenatal and postpartum behaviors|journal=Contraception|volume=79|issue=3|pages=194–8|doi=10.1016/j.contraception.2008.09.009|pmid=19185672}}
|4={{cite journal|vauthors=Kost K, Landry D, Darroch J|date=Mar–Apr 1998|title=Predicting maternal behaviors during pregnancy: Does intention status matter?|journal=Fam Plann Perspectives|volume=30|issue=2|pages=79–88|doi=10.2307/2991664|jstor=2991664|pmid=9561873}}
|5={{cite journal|vauthors=D'Angelo DV, Gilbert BC, Rochat RW, Santelli JS, Herold JM|date=Sep–Oct 2004|title=Differences between mistimed and unwanted pregnancies among women who have live births|journal=Perspect Sex Reprod Health|volume=36|issue=5|pages=192–7|doi=10.1363/3619204|pmid=15519961}}
}}</ref>

===Peer influence===
[[Social rejection|Peer rejection]] in childhood is also a large predictor of juvenile delinquency.<ref name="Bartol2" /> This rejection can affect the child's ability to be [[Socialization|socialized]] properly and often leads them to gravitate towards anti-social peer groups.<ref name="Bartol2" /> Association with anti-social groups often leads to the promotion of violent, aggressive and deviant behavior.<ref name="Bartol2" /> Robert Vargas's "Being in 'Bad' Company," explains that adolescents who can choose between groups of friends are less susceptible to peer influence that could lead them to commit illegal acts.<ref>{{Cite journal|last=Vargas|first=Robert|date=September 2011|title=Being in "Bad" Company: Power Dependence and Status in Adolescent Susceptibility to Peer Influence|journal=Social Psychology Quarterly|volume=74|issue=3|pages=310–332|doi=10.1177/0190272511414546|s2cid=147382098|issn=0190-2725}}</ref> Aggressive adolescents who have been rejected by peers are also more likely to have a "hostile attribution bias", which leads people to interpret the actions of others (whether they be hostile or not) as purposefully hostile and aggressive towards them.<ref name="Dodge2003">{{cite journal|last1=Dodge|year=2003|title=A biopsychosocial model of the development of chronic conduct problems in adolescence|journal=Developmental Psychology|volume=39|issue=2|pages=349–371|doi=10.1037/0012-1649.39.2.349|pmc=2755613|pmid=12661890}}</ref> This often leads to an impulsive and aggressive reaction.<ref name="Dodge2003" />

[[Conformity]] plays a significant role in the vast impact that peer group influence has on an individual. Aronson, Wilson, & Akert (2013)<ref name="Aronson2013">{{Cite book|last=Aronson, Elliot.|title=Social psychology|date=2013|publisher=Pearson|others=Wilson, Timothy D., Akert, Robin M.|isbn=9780205796625|edition=8th|location=Boston|oclc=796355014}}</ref> point to the research experiment conducted by [[Solomon Asch's experiment|Solomon Asch]] (1956),<ref name="Asch1956">{{Cite journal|last=Asch|first=Solomon E.|date=1956|title=Studies of independence and conformity: I. A minority of one against a unanimous majority.|journal=Psychological Monographs: General and Applied|volume=70|issue=9|pages=1–70|doi=10.1037/h0093718|s2cid=144985742 |issn=0096-9753}}</ref> to ascertain whether a group could influence an individual's behavior. The experiment was executed by asking a participant to determine which line in the set of 3 lines matched the length of an original line. Confederates knew the purpose of the experiment and were directed to answer the questions incorrectly during certain phases of the experiment. These confederates answered the question before the participant. The confederates answered the first few questions correctly, as did the participant. Eventually, all of the confederates started to answer incorrectly. The purpose of the experiment was to see if the group would influence the participant to answer incorrectly. Asch found that seventy-six percent of the participants conformed and answered incorrectly when influenced by the group.<ref name="Asch1956" /> According to these findings, it was concluded that a peer group that is involved in deviant behavior can influence an adolescent to engage in similar activities.<ref>{{Cite journal|last1=Kreager|first1=Derek A.|last2=Rulison|first2=Kelly|last3=Moody|first3=James|title=Delinquency and the Structure of Adolescent Peer Groups|date=February 2011|journal=Criminology|volume=49|issue=1|pages=95–127|doi=10.1111/j.1745-9125.2010.00219.x|issn=0011-1384|pmc=3092163|pmid=21572969}}</ref> Once the adolescent becomes part of the group, they will be susceptible to [[groupthink]].<ref name="Aronson2013" />

===School to prison pipeline===
A common contributor to juvenile delinquency rates is a phenomenon referred to as the [[School-to-prison pipeline|school to prison pipeline]]. In recent years, school disciplinary measures have become increasingly policed.<ref name="Theriot2009">{{Cite journal|last=Theriot|first=Matthew T.|date=1 May 2009|title=School resource officers and the criminalization of student behavior|journal=Journal of Criminal Justice|language=en|volume=37|issue=3|pages=280–287|doi=10.1016/j.jcrimjus.2009.04.008|issn=0047-2352}}</ref> According to one study, 67% of high school students attend schools with police officers.<ref>{{Cite journal|url=https://www.urban.org/urban-wire/prevalence-police-officers-us-schools|title=The prevalence of police officers in US schools|last1=Lindsay|first1=Constance A.|last2=Lee|first2=Victoria|date=21 June 2018|journal=Urban Institute|language=en|access-date=8 March 2020|last3=Lloyd|first3=Tracey}}</ref> This rise in police presence is often attributed to the implementation of [[Zero tolerance (schools)|zero tolerance policies]].<ref name="sharedjustice20171221">{{Cite web|url=http://www.sharedjustice.org/domestic-justice/2017/12/21/zero-tolerance-policies-and-the-school-to-prison-pipeline|title=Zero-Tolerance Policies and the School to Prison Pipeline|website=Shared Justice|language=en-US|access-date=8 March 2020}}</ref> Based on the [[Broken windows theory|"broken windows" theory]] of criminology and the [[Gun-Free Schools Act of 1994|Gun-Free Schools Act]], zero tolerance policies stress the use of specific, consistent, and harsh punishment to deal with in school infractions.<ref>{{Cite journal|date=April 2008|title=Are Zero Tolerance Policies Effective in the Schools? An Evidentiary Review and Recommendations|url=https://www.apa.org/pubs/info/reports/zero-tolerance.pdf|journal=American Psychologist}}</ref><ref name="sharedjustice20171221" /> Often measures such as suspension or expulsion are assigned to students who deviant regardless of the reason or past disciplinary history.<ref name="sharedjustice20171221" /> This use of punishment often has been linked with increasing high school drop out rates and future arrests.<ref name="Fowler2010">{{Cite journal|last=Fowler|first=Deborah|date=April 2010|title=Texas' School-to-Prison Pipeline: School Expulsion, The Path from Lockout to Dropout|url=http://www.njjn.org/uploads/digital-library/Texas-School-Prison-Pipeline-School-Expulsion_Texas-Appleseed_Apr2010.pdf|journal=Texas Appleseed|archive-date=25 June 2021|access-date=8 March 2020|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20210625125530/http://www.njjn.org/uploads/digital-library/Texas-School-Prison-Pipeline-School-Expulsion_Texas-Appleseed_Apr2010.pdf|url-status=dead}}</ref> It was found in a 2018 study that students who received a suspension were less likely to graduate and more likely to be arrested or on probation.<ref>{{Cite journal|last=Rosenbaum|first=Janet|date=17 January 2018|title=Educational and Criminal Justice Outcomes 12 Years After School Suspension|journal=Youth & Society|volume=52|issue=4|language=en-US|pages=515–547|doi=10.1177/0044118x17752208|pmid=32528191|issn=0044-118X|pmc=7288849}}</ref> As stated in research by Matthew Theriot, the increased police presence in school and use of tougher punishment methods leads student actions to be criminalized and in turn referred to juvenile justice systems.<ref name="Theriot2009" />

The Center on Youth Justice at the Vera Institute of Justice found that "for similar students attending similar schools, a single suspension or expulsion doubles the risk that a student will repeat a grade.<ref>{{Cite journal|date=December 2013|title=A Generation Later: What We've Learned about Zero Tolerance in Schools|url=https://storage.googleapis.com/vera-web-assets/downloads/Publications/a-generation-later-what-weve-learned-about-zero-tolerance-in-schools/legacy_downloads/zero-tolerance-in-schools-policy-brief.pdf|journal=Vera Institute of Justice}}</ref> Being retained a grade, especially while in middle or high school, is one of the strongest predictors of dropping out.<ref name="sharedjustice20171221" /> In a national longitudinal study, it was reported that youth with a prior suspension were 68% more likely to dropout of school.<ref name="sharedjustice20171221" />

The school to prison pipeline disproportionately affects minority students.<ref>{{Cite journal|last1=Morris|first1=Edward W.|last2=Perry|first2=Brea L.|date=1 February 2016|title=The Punishment Gap: School Suspension and Racial Disparities in Achievement|url=https://academic.oup.com/socpro/article/63/1/68/1844875|journal=Social Problems|language=en|volume=63|issue=1|pages=68–86|doi=10.1093/socpro/spv026|issn=0037-7791|url-access=subscription}}</ref> According to data compiled by the United States Government Accountability Office, 39% of students who received a suspension in the 2013–14 school year were Black, even though Black students accounted for only about 15% of public school students.<ref name="gao2018">{{Cite web|url=http://www.gao.gov/assets/700/690828.pdf|title=Discipline Disparities for Black Students, Boys, and Students with Disabilities|publisher=United States Government Accountability Office|date=March 2018}}</ref> This over-representation applied to both boys and girls of African descent.<ref name="gao2018" /> Compared to White students, Black students were expelled or suspended 3 times as frequently.<ref>{{Cite web|url=http://www.justicepolicy.org/mobile/news/8775|title=The school to prison pipeline, explained — Justice Policy Institute|website=justicepolicy.org|date=24 February 2015|access-date=8 March 2020}}</ref>

==Personality factors==
Juvenile delinquency is the unlawful activities by minors in their teen or pre-teen years. It is influenced by four main risk factors, namely: personality, background, state of mind and drugs.

===Gender===
[[Gender]] is another risk factor in regards to influencing delinquent behavior. The predictors of different types of delinquency vary across females and males for various reasons, but a common underlying reason for this is [[socialization]].<ref name="Junger-Tas 333–375">{{Cite journal|last1=Junger-Tas|first1=Josine|last2=Ribeaud|first2=Denis|last3=Cruyff|first3=Maarten J. L. F.|date=July 2004|title=Juvenile Delinquency and Gender|journal=European Journal of Criminology|volume=1|issue=3|pages=333–375|doi=10.1177/1477370804044007|s2cid=145375000|issn=1477-3708}}</ref><ref name="Steketee-Junger-Junger-Tas-2013">{{Cite journal|last1=Steketee|first1=Majone|last2=Junger|first2=Marianne|last3=Junger-Tas|first3=Josine|date=20 January 2013|title=Sex Differences in the Predictors of Juvenile Delinquency|url=https://ris.utwente.nl/ws/files/145893868/Steketee2013sex.pdf|journal=Journal of Contemporary Criminal Justice|language=en-US|volume=29|issue=1|pages=88–105|doi=10.1177/1043986212470888|s2cid=143911396|issn=1043-9862|url-access=|url-status=|archive-url=|archive-date=}}</ref> Different predictors of delinquency emerge when analyzing distinct offending types across gender, but overall it is evident that males commit more crimes than females.<ref>{{Cite journal|last1=Daigle|first1=Leah E.|last2=Cullen|first2=Francis T.|last3=Wright|first3=John Paul|date=July 2007|title=Gender Differences in the Predictors of Juvenile Delinquency|journal=Youth Violence and Juvenile Justice|language=en-US|volume=5|issue=3|pages=254–286|doi=10.1177/1541204007301289|s2cid=144071237|issn=1541-2040}}</ref> Across all offenses, females are less likely to be involved in delinquent acts than males.<ref name="Junger-Tas 333–375" /> Females not only commit fewer offenses, but they also commit less serious offenses.<ref name="Steketee-Junger-Junger-Tas-2013" />

Socialization plays a key role in the gender gap in delinquency because male and female juveniles are often socialized differently. Girls' and boys' experiences are heavily mediated by gender, which alters their interactions in society. Males and females are  differently controlled and bonded, suggesting that they will not make the same choices and may follow different paths of delinquency. Social bonds are important for both males and females, but different aspects of the bond are relevant for each gender.<ref>{{Cite journal|last=Svensson|first=Robert|date=October 2004|title=Shame as a Consequence of the Parent-Child Relationship|journal=European Journal of Criminology|volume=1|issue=4|pages=477–504|doi=10.1177/1477370804045692|s2cid=145803713|issn=1477-3708}}</ref> The degree of involvement in social settings is a significant predictor of male's violent delinquency, but is not significant for females. Males tend to be more connected with their peer relationships which in effect has a stronger influence on their behavior.<ref name="RhoadesLeveEddyChamblerlain2016">{{Cite journal|last1=Rhoades|first1=Kimberly A.|last2=Leve|first2=Leslie D.|last3=Eddy|first3=J. Mark|last4=Chamberlain|first4=Patricia|date=December 2016|title=Predicting the transition from juvenile delinquency to adult criminality: Gender-specific influences in two high-risk samples: Recidivism from adolescence to adulthood|journal=Criminal Behaviour and Mental Health|language=en|volume=26|issue=5|pages=336–351|doi=10.1002/cbm.1957|pmc=4624625|pmid=25916547}}</ref><ref name="PiqueroGoverMacDonaldPiquero2005">{{Cite journal|last1=Piquero|first1=Nicole Leeper|last2=Gover|first2=Angela R.|last3=MacDonald|first3=John M.|last4=Piquero|first4=Alex R.|date=March 2005|title=The Influence of Delinquen Peers on Delinquency|journal=Youth & Society|language=en-US|volume=36|issue=3|pages=251–275|doi=10.1177/0044118x04265652|s2cid=144907955|issn=0044-118X}}</ref> Association with delinquent peers is one of the strongest correlates of juvenile delinquency, and much of the gender gap can be accounted for by the fact that males are more likely to have friends that support delinquent behavior. Delinquent peers are positively and significantly related to delinquency in males but delinquent peers are negatively and insignificantly related to delinquency for females.<ref name="PiqueroGoverMacDonaldPiquero2005" /> As for females, familial functioning relationships have shown to be more important. Female juveniles tend to be more strongly connected with their families, the disconnect or the lack of socialization between their family members can significantly predict their likelihood of committing crimes as juveniles and even as adults. When the family is disrupted, females are more likely to engage in delinquent behavior than males.<ref name="Steketee-Junger-Junger-Tas-2013" /> Boys, however, tend to be less connected to their family and are not as affected by these relationships.<ref name="RhoadesLeveEddyChamblerlain2016" /> When it comes to minor offenses such as fighting, vandalism, shoplifting, and the carrying of weapons, differences in gender are limited because they are most common among both males as well as females. Elements of the social bond, social disorganization, routine activities, opportunity, and attitudes towards violence are also related to delinquent behavior among both males and females.<ref name="Steketee-Junger-Junger-Tas-2013" />

===Neurological===
Individual psychological or behavioral risk factors that may make offending more likely include low [[intelligence]], [[impulsiveness]] or the inability to delay [[gratification]], [[aggression]], lack of [[empathy]], and [[anxiety|restlessness]].<ref name="Farrington2" /> Other risk factors that may be evident during childhood and adolescence include, aggressive or troublesome behavior, language delays or impairments, lack of emotional control (learning to control one's anger), and cruelty to animals.<ref name="Bartol2" />

Children with [[low intelligence]] are more likely to do badly in [[school]]. This may increase the chances of offending because low educational attainment, a low attachment to school, and low educational aspirations are all risk factors for offending in themselves.<ref name="Walklate2" /><ref>{{cite news|url=http://journalistsresource.org/studies/society/education/juvenile-arrest-collateral-educational-damage/|title=Juvenile Arrest and Collateral Educational Damage in the Transition to Adulthood}} JournalistsResource.org, retrieved 18 June 2012</ref><ref>{{cite journal|last1=Kirk|first1=David S.|last2=Sampson|first2=Robert J.|year=2012|title=Juvenile Arrest and Collateral Educational Damage in the Transition to Adulthood|journal=[[Sociology of Education (journal)|Sociology of Education]]|volume=86|issue=1|pages=36–62|doi=10.1177/0038040712448862|pmc=4192649|pmid=25309003}}</ref> Children who perform poorly at school are also more likely to be [[truant]], and the status offense of truancy is linked to further offending.<ref name="Farrington2" />

Impulsiveness is seen by some as the key aspect of a child's [[Personality psychology|personality]] that predicts offending.<ref name="Farrington2" /> However, it is not clear whether these aspects of personality are a result of "deficits in the executive functions of the [[Human brain|brain]]"<ref name="Farrington2" /> or a result of parental influences or other social factors.<ref name="Graham4" /> In any event, studies of adolescent development show that teenagers are more prone to [[Adolescence#Risk-taking|risk-taking]], which may explain the high disproportionate rate of offending among adolescents.<ref name="SteinbergAdolescence2"/>

===Psychological===
Juvenile delinquents are often diagnosed with different disorders. Around six to sixteen percent of male teens and two to nine percent of female teens have a conduct disorder. These can vary from [[oppositional-defiant disorder]], which is not necessarily aggressive, to [[antisocial personality disorder]], often diagnosed among [[psychopaths]].<ref>{{cite book|last=Hare|title=The Hare Psychopathy Checklist Revised|publisher=Multi-Health Systems|year=1991|location=Toronto, Ontario}}</ref> A [[conduct disorder]] can develop during childhood and then manifest itself during adolescence.<ref>{{cite journal|last1=Holmes|first1=S. E.|last2=James|first2=R. S.|last3=Javad|first3=K.|year=2001|title=Risk Factors in Childhood that Lead to the Development of Conduct Disorder and Antisocial Personality Disorder|journal=Child Psychiatry and Human Development|volume=31|issue=3|pages=183–193|doi=10.1023/A:1026425304480|pmid=11196010|s2cid=20259352}}</ref>

Juvenile delinquents who have recurring encounters with the criminal justice system, or in other words those who are life-course-persistent offenders, are sometimes diagnosed with [[conduct disorder]]s because they show a continuous disregard for their own and others safety and/or property. Once the juvenile continues to exhibit the same behavioral patterns and turns eighteen he is then at risk of being diagnosed with antisocial personality disorder and much more prone to become a serious criminal offender.<ref name="DeLisi">{{cite book|last=DeLisi|first=Matt|title=Career Criminals in Society|url=https://archive.org/details/careerc_del_2005_00_4404|url-access=registration|publisher=Sage Publications|year=2005|isbn=978-1412905534|location=London, United Kingdom|page=[https://archive.org/details/careerc_del_2005_00_4404/page/39 39]}}</ref> One of the main components used in diagnosing an adult with antisocial personality disorder consists of presenting documented history of conduct disorder before the age of 15. These two personality disorders are analogous in their erratic and aggressive behavior. This is why habitual juvenile offenders diagnosed with conduct disorder are likely to exhibit signs of antisocial personality disorder early in life and then as they mature. Some times these juveniles reach maturation and they develop into career criminals, or life-course-persistent offenders. "Career criminals begin committing antisocial behavior before entering grade school and are versatile in that they engage in an array of destructive behaviors, offend at exceedingly high rates, and are less likely to quit committing crime as they age."<ref name="DeLisi" />

Quantitative research was completed on 9,945 juvenile male offenders between the ages of 10 and 18 in Philadelphia, Pennsylvania in the 1970s.<ref name="Marvin">{{cite book|last1=Marvin|first1=Wolfgang|url=https://archive.org/details/delinquencyinbir0000wolf|title=Delinquency in a Birth Cohort|last2=Figlio|first2=Robert M.|last3=Sellin|first3=Thorsten|publisher=University of Chicago Press|year=1972|isbn=978-0226905532|url-access=registration}}</ref> The longitudinal birth cohort was used to examine a trend among a small percentage of career criminals who accounted for the largest percentage of crime activity.<ref name="Marvin" /> The trend exhibited a new phenomenon among habitual offenders. The phenomenon indicated that only 6% of the youth qualified under their definition of a habitual offender (known today as life-course persistent offenders, or career criminals) and yet were responsible for 52% of the delinquency within the entire study.<ref name="Marvin" /> The same 6% of chronic offenders accounted for 71% of the murders and 69% of the aggravated assaults.<ref name="Marvin" /> This phenomenon was later researched among an adult population in 1977 and resulted in similar findings. S. A. Mednick did a birth cohort of 30,000 males and found that 1% of the males were responsible for more than half of the criminal activity.<ref>{{cite book|last=Raine|first=A.|title=The Psychopathology of Crime: Criminal Behavior as a Clinical Disorder|publisher=Academic Press|year=1993|isbn=978-0125761604|location=San Diego, California}}</ref> The habitual crime behavior found among juveniles is similar to that of adults. As stated before most life-course persistent offenders begin exhibiting antisocial, violent, and/or delinquent behavior, prior to adolescence. Therefore, while there is a high rate of juvenile delinquency, it is the small percentage of life-course persistent, career criminals that are responsible for most of the violent crimes.

==Criminology==
There are a multitude of different theories on the causes of crime ([[criminology]]) most, if not all, of which are applicable to the causes of juvenile delinquency.

===Rational choice===
Classical criminology stresses that the causes of crime lie within [[individual]] offenders, rather than in their external environment. For classicists, offenders are motivated by [[rationality|rational]] [[wikt:self-interest|self-interest]], and the importance of [[free will]] and personal [[moral responsibility|responsibility]] is emphasized.<ref name="Eadie">{{cite book |last1=Eadie |first1=T. |last2=Morley |first2=R. |year=2003 |chapter=Crime, Justice and Punishment |editor-last=Baldock |editor-first=J. |title=Social Policy |edition= 3rd |location=Oxford |publisher=Oxford University Press |isbn=978-0199258949 |display-editors=etal}}</ref> [[Rational choice theory (criminology)|Rational choice theory]] is the clearest example of that idea. Delinquency is one of the major factors motivated by rational choice.

===Social disorganization===
Current [[Positivist criminology|positivist approaches]] generally focus on the [[culture]]. A type of criminological theory attributing variation in crime and delinquency over time and among territories to the absence or breakdown of communal institutions (such as family, school, church, and social groups) and communal relationships that traditionally encouraged cooperative relationships among people.

===Strain===
[[Strain theory (sociology)|Strain theory]] is associated mainly with the work of [[Robert K. Merton]], who felt that there are [[institutionalization|institutionalized]] paths to success in [[society]]. Strain theory holds that crime is caused by the difficulty for those in [[poverty]] have to achieve socially-valued goals by legitimate means.<ref name="Eadie" /> Since those with, for instance, poor educational attainment have difficulty achieving wealth and status by securing well-paid employment, they are more likely to use criminal means to obtain those goals.<ref name="Brown">Brown, S (1998) ''Understanding Youth and Crime (Listening to youth?)'', Buckingham: Open University Press.</ref>
Merton's suggests five adaptations to this dilemma:
# ''Innovation'': individuals who accept socially-approved goals but not necessarily the socially-approved means.
# ''Retreatism'': those who reject socially-approved goals and the means for acquiring them.
# ''Ritualism'': those who buy into a system of socially-approved means but lose sight of the goals. Merton believed that drug users are in this category.
# ''Conformity'': those who conform to the system's means and goals.
# ''Rebellion'': people who negate socially-approved goals and means by creating a new system of acceptable goals and means.

A difficulty with strain theory is that it does not explore why children of low-income families have poor educational attainment in the first place. More importantly, much youth crime does not have an economic motivation. Strain theory fails to explain [[Violent crime#Types|violent crime]], the type of youth crime that causes most anxiety to the public.

===Differential association===
[[Differential association]] is another theory that deals with young people in a group context and looks at how [[peer pressure]] and the existence of gangs could lead them into crime. It suggests young people are motivated to commit crimes by delinquent peers and learn criminal skills from them. The diminished influence of peers after men [[marriage|marry]] has also been cited as a factor in desisting from offending. There is strong evidence that young people with criminal friends are more likely to commit crimes themselves. However, offenders may prefer to associate with one another, rather than delinquent peers causing someone to start offending. Furthermore, there is the question of how the delinquent peer group initially became delinquent.

===Labeling===
[[Labeling theory]] is a concept in criminology that aims to explain deviant behavior from the social context, rather the individual themselves. It is part of interactionism criminology, which states that once young people have been labeled as criminal, they are more likely to offend.<ref name="Eadie" /> The idea is that once labelled as deviant, a young person may accept that [[role]] and be more likely to associate with others who have been similarly labeled.<ref name="Eadie" /> Labelling theorists say that male children from poor families are more likely to be labelled deviant, which may partially explain the existence of more [[working-class]] young male offenders.<ref name="Walklate2" />

===Social control===
[[Social control theory]] proposes that exploiting the process of [[socialization]] and social learning builds [[self-control]] and can reduce the inclination to indulge in behavior that is recognized as antisocial. These four types of control can help prevent juvenile delinquency:

'''Direct''' by which punishment is threatened or applied for wrongful behavior, and compliance is rewarded by parents, family, and authority figures.
'''Internal''' by which a youth refrains from delinquency through the conscience or superego.
'''Indirect''' by identification with those who influence behavior, such as because the delinquent act might cause pain and disappointment to parents and others close relationships.
Control through needs satisfaction: if all an individual's needs are met, there is no point in criminal activity.

==Punishment==
In 2020 a ruling abolished the death penalty for juveniles in Saudi Arabia. Despite this Mustafa Hashem al-Darwish was executed in June 2021. He was alleged to have of taken part in anti-government demonstrations at the age of 17.  al-Darwish had been detained in May 2015 being placed in [[solitary confinement]] for years. al-Darwish claimed that he faced brutal torture and beatings and was forced to sign confessions.<ref>{{cite news|url=https://www.thetimes.co.uk/article/mustafa-hashem-al-darwish-saudi-arabia-executes-man-who-took-part-in-protests-when-under-18-bz0zlpjgc|title=Mustafa Hashem al-Darwish: Saudi Arabia executes man who took part in protests when under 18|access-date=16 June 2021|work=The Times}}</ref><ref>{{cite web|url=https://www.npr.org/2021/06/18/1007778068/saudi-arabia-executed-a-man-for-childhood-offenses-at-least-9-others-could-be-ne?t=1624513378403|title=A Man Was Executed For An Alleged Crime When He Was A Teen. More Saudis Could Follow|website=NPR |date=18 June 2021 |accessdate=18 June 2021|publisher=National Public Radio|last1=Faheid |first1=Dalia }}</ref>

One [[criminal justice]] approach to juvenile delinquency is through the [[juvenile court]] systems. These courts are specifically for minors to be tried in. Sometimes, juvenile offenders are sent to adult prisons.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.hrw.org/topic/childrens-rights/juvenile-justice|title=Juvenile Justice|website=Human Rights Watch|language=en|access-date=8 March 2020}}</ref> In the United States, children as young as 8 can be tried and convicted as adults. Additionally, the United States was the only recorded country to sentence children as young as 13 to life sentences without parole also known as death in prison sentences. As of 2012, the Supreme Court has declared death in prison sentences unconstitutional for the vast majority of cases involving children.<ref name="eji">{{Cite web|url=https://eji.org/issues/children-in-prison/|title=Children in Adult Prison|website=Equal Justice Initiative|language=en-US|access-date=20 April 2020}}</ref> According to the US Department of Justice, about 3,600 children are housed in adult jails.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.ojjdp.gov/ojstatbb/corrections/qa08700.asp|title=Jail inmates younger than 18, 1993-2017|website=ojjdp.gov|access-date=8 March 2020}}</ref>

According to a report released by the Prison Policy Initiative, over 48,000 children are held in [[juvenile detention centers]] or prisons in America.<ref name="youth2019">{{Cite press release |url=https://www.prisonpolicy.org/reports/youth2019.html |title=Youth Confinement: The Whole Pie 2019 |last1=Sawyer |first1=Wendy |publisher=Prison Policy Initiative |date=19 December 2019 |language=en |access-date=20 April 2020}}</ref> The worldwide number is unknown but UNICEF estimates that over 1 million children experience confinement in various countries.<ref>{{Cite book|chapter-url=https://www.hrw.org/world-report/2016/children-behind-bars|title=World Report 2016: Rights Trends in Children Behind Bars|chapter=Children Behind Bars: The Global Overuse of Detention of Children |date=22 December 2015|language=en|access-date=20 April 2020}}</ref> Juveniles in youth detention centers are sometimes subject to many of the same punishments as adults, such as solitary confinement, despite a younger age or the presence of disabilities.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.theatlantic.com/politics/archive/2017/12/juvenile-solitary-confinement/548933/|title=What If This Were Your Kid?|last=McCluskey|first=Molly|date=24 December 2017|website=The Atlantic|language=en-US|access-date=20 April 2020}}</ref> Due to the influx of minors in detention facilities due to the school to prison pipeline, education is increasingly becoming a concern. Children in juvenile detention have a compromised or nonexistent schooling which to a higher number of drop outs and failure to complete secondary education.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.aclu.org/sites/default/files/field_document/final_11-million-days_ucla_aclu.|title=11 Million Days Lost: RACE, DISCIPLINE, AND SAFETY AT U.S. PUBLIC SCHOOLS|last=Losen|first=Daniel|date=24 August 2018|website=The Center for Civil Rights Remedies}}{{Dead link|date=February 2025 |bot=InternetArchiveBot |fix-attempted=yes }}</ref>

==Prevention==
[[File:Cure juvenile delinquency in the slums by planned housing 3b48917r (6288776732).jpg|thumb|1936 poster promoting planned housing as a method to deter juvenile delinquency, showing silhouettes of a child stealing a piece of fruit and an older child involved in armed robbery]]

Delinquency prevention is the broad term for all efforts aimed at preventing youth from becoming involved in criminal, or other antisocial, activity. Prevention services may include activities such as substance abuse education and treatment, family counseling, youth mentoring, parenting education, educational support, and youth sheltering. Increasing availability and use of [[family planning]] services, including education and [[Birth control|contraceptives]] helps to reduce [[unintended pregnancy]] and unwanted births, which are risk factors for delinquency. It has been noted that often interventions such as peer groups may leave at-risk children worse off than if there had never been an intervention.<ref name="DishionMcCord2">{{cite journal|last1=Dishion|last2=McCord|year=1999|title=When interventions harm :Peer groups and problem behavior|journal=American Psychologist|volume=54|issue=9|pages=755–764|doi=10.1037/0003-066x.54.9.755|pmid=10510665}}</ref>

===Policies===
[[Education]] promotes economic growth, national productivity and innovation, and values of democracy and social cohesion.<ref name="education and development2">{{cite web|url=http://go.worldbank.org/f5k8Y429go|title=Education and development|author1=World Bank|url-status=live|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20170129174645/http://go.worldbank.org/f5k8Y429go|archive-date=29 January 2017|access-date=18 January 2012}}</ref> Prevention through education has been seen to discourage delinquency for minors and help them strengthen the connection and understanding between peers<ref>{{Cite journal|last1=Katsiyannis|first1=Antonis|last2=Ryan|first2=Joseph B.|last3=Zhang|first3=Dalun|last4=Spann|first4=Anastasia|date=4 March 2008|title=Juvenile Delinquency and Recidivism: The Impact of Academic Achievement|journal=Reading & Writing Quarterly|volume=24|issue=2|pages=177–196|doi=10.1080/10573560701808460|s2cid=144089622|issn=1057-3569}}</ref>

A well-known intervention treatment is the Scared Straight Treatment.<ref>{{Cite journal|last1=Petrosino|first1=A|last2=Turpin-Petrosino|first2=Carolyn|last3=Hollis-Peel|first3=Meghan|last4=Lavenberg|first4=Julia|date=2013|title=Scared Straight and Other Juvenile Awareness Programs for Preventing Juvenile Delinquency: A Systematic Review|url=https://campbellcollaboration.org/library/juvenile-delinquency-scared-straight-etc-programmes|journal=Campbell Systematic Reviews|volume=5|pages=1–55|doi=10.4073/csr.2013.5|doi-access=free|url-access=subscription}}</ref> According to research done by Scott Lilienfeld, this type of intervention is often harmful because of juvenile offenders' vicarious exposure to criminal role models and the possibility of increased resentment in reaction to the confrontational interactions.<ref>{{cite journal|last1=Lilienfeld|first1=Scott O.|year=2007|title=Psychological Treatments That Cause Harm|journal=Perspectives on Psychological Science|volume=2|issue=1|pages=53–70|citeseerx=10.1.1.531.9405|doi=10.1111/j.1745-6916.2007.00029.x|pmid=26151919|s2cid=26512757}}</ref> It has been reasoned that the most efficient interventions are those that not only separate at-risk teens from anti-social peers, and place them instead with pro-social ones, but also simultaneously improve their home environment by training parents with appropriate parenting styles.<ref name="DishionMcCord2" />

In response to the data correlated with the school to prison pipeline, some institutions have implemented [[restorative justice]] policies.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.iirp.edu/pdf/IIRP-Improving-School-Climate.pdf|title=Improving School Climate: Evidence from Schools Implementing Restorative Practices|publisher=International Institute for Restorative Practices|date=2014}}</ref> The restorative justice approach emphasizes conflict resolution and non-punitive intervention.<ref name="Fronius2016">{{Cite journal|last=Fronius|first=Trevor|date=Feb 2016|title=Restorative Justice in U.S. Schools: A Research Review|url=https://jprc.wested.org/wp-content/uploads/2016/02/RJ_Literature-Review_20160217.pdf|journal=WestEd Justice & Prevention Research Center|archive-date=12 December 2019|access-date=8 March 2020|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20191212094022/https://jprc.wested.org/wp-content/uploads/2016/02/RJ_Literature-Review_20160217.pdf|url-status=dead}}</ref> Interventions such as hiring more counselors as opposed to security professionals or focusing on talking through problems would be included in a restorative justice approach.<ref name="Fronius2016" />

It is also important to note certain works of legislation that have already been published in the United States in response to general prisoner re-entry, extending to juveniles, such as the [[Second Chance Act (2007)]] and most recently, the [https://www.congress.gov/bill/115th-congress/senate-bill/3635/all-info Second Chance Reauthorization Act (2018).]<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.congress.gov/bill/115th-congress/senate-bill/3635/all-info|title=All Info – S.3635 – 115th Congress (2017–2018): Second Chance Reauthorization Act of 2018|last=Portman|first=Rob|date=15 November 2018|website=congress.gov|access-date=15 April 2020}}</ref>

===Juvenile reform===
Juvenile reform deals with the vocational programs and educational approach to reducing recidivism rates of juvenile offenders. Most countries in the world legislate processes for juvenile reform and re-entry, some more elaborate and formal than others. In theory, juvenile re-entry is sensitive to the fact that juveniles are young and assumes they are capable of change; it approaches a juvenile offender's situation and history holistically, evaluating the earlier factors that could lead a juvenile to commit crimes. In practice, this is complicated since juvenile delinquents return home to varying and unpredictable circumstances, including poverty, substance abuse, domestic violence, etc..

In the United States, juvenile reform is split into four main phases:<ref>{{Cite web|title=Reentry {{!}} Youth.gov|url=https://youth.gov/youth-topics/juvenile-justice/reentry#_ftn1|website=youth.gov|access-date=11 May 2020}}</ref>

# The Entry Phase: The youth enters residential placement
# The Placement Phase: Amount of time youth is in the placement facility (whatever that may be)
# The Transitional Phase (re-entry): Act of leaving facility and entering community (from right after exit of facility to right before entering community)
# The Community-based Aftercare Phase: Period of time after youth returns to the community (usually 120-day period right after transitional phase)

An understanding of the factors involved in each of these steps is crucial to creating an effective juvenile reform program. One non-profit identifies the following approaches to juvenile reform:<ref>{{Cite web|title=Reform Trends|url=https://jjie.org/hub/community-based-alternatives/reform-trends/|website=Juvenile Justice Information Exchange|language=en-US|access-date=11 May 2020}}</ref>

# Early Intervention: preventing juvenile youth from ever encountering the justice system by implementation of  conflict-resolution practices or administrative strategies that aim to teach the child healthy actions to take in difficult situations. It is implemented before any offense is committed and often involves a thorough discussion of what individual issues a child is dealing with.
# Diversion: the placement of youth in programs that redirect youth away from juvenile justice system processing, or programs that divert youth from secure detention in a juvenile justice facility. These programs are most often in attempt to protect juveniles from getting a charge on their record after they have already committed a crime. This can be led through school administration intervention or by law enforcement officers that have been trained in dealing with at-risk youth. These programs are often given to children who have unstable life circumstances and are thus extended aid that will attack the "root problems" rather than further isolate them in society.
# Alternatives to Secure Confinement: a juvenile justice approach that does not require the juvenile's entry in a "jail-like" facility. Often involves the juvenile's continued participation in society, but in a modified manner. Such alternatives include home confinement, supervision of a probation officer, community service requirements, and community-based facilities, among others.
# Evidence-Based Practices: the emphasis on encouraging youth participation in programs that have evidence of working. The evaluation of "success" for a program is dependent on multiple factors, such as reduction of recidivism rates, cost-effectiveness, and addressing health problems.
# Diverting Youth Who Commit Status Offenses: programs that address the "root" problems causing a juvenile's behavior and actions. Such programs are often part of a tiered approach to juvenile justice and reform.
# Funding Community-Based Alternatives on a Large Scale: the supporting of all initiatives in a community that have been proven to help with juvenile betterment and reform. This allows the community to help its own and does not rely on the decisions of the state regarding the needs of juveniles.

While juvenile reform has proved to be an effective and humanizing approach response to juvenile delinquency, it is a very complex area that still has many ongoing debates. For example, many countries around the world are debating the appropriate age of a juvenile, as well as trying to understand whether there are some crimes that are so heinous, they should be exempt from any understanding. Based on these discussions, legislation needs to be consistently updated and considered as social, cultural, and political landscapes change.

==Juvenile sex crimes==
{{globalize|section|USA|date=September 2022}}

[[Minor (law)|Juveniles]] who commit sexual crimes refer to individuals adjudicated in a [[Criminal law|criminal court]] for a sexual crime.<ref name="Barbaree">Barbaree, H. E., Marshall, W. L. (2008). An introduction to the juvenile sex offender: Terms, concepts, and definitions (2nd Ed.). New York: Guilford Press.</ref> Sex crimes are defined as sexually abusive behavior committed by a person under the age of 18 that is perpetrated "against the victim's will, without consent, and in an aggressive, exploitative, manipulative, and/or threatening manner".<ref name="Ryan">Ryan, G., Lane, S. (Eds.). (1997). Juvenile Sexual Offending: Causes consequences and correction. San Francisco: Jossey-Bass.</ref> It is important to utilize appropriate terminology for juvenile sex offenders. Harsh and inappropriate expressions include terms such as "[[pedophile]], [[child molester]], [[predator]], perpetrator, and mini-perp".<ref name="Longo">Longo, R. E., Prescott, D. S. (2006). Introduction: A brief history of treating youth with sexual behavior problems. Current perspectives: Working with sexually aggressive youth and youth with sexual behavior problems, (pp. 31–43). Massachusetts: NEARI Press.</ref> These terms have often been associated with this group, regardless of the youth's age, [[diagnosis]], [[cognitive abilities]], or [[Child development stages|developmental stage]].<ref name="Longo"/> Using appropriate expressions can facilitate a more accurate depiction of juvenile sex offenders and may decrease the subsequent aversive psychological affects from using such labels.<ref name="Longo"/> In the [[Arab states of the Persian Gulf]], [[homosexual acts]] are classified as an offense, and constitute one of the primary crimes for which juvenile males are charged.<ref name="Booth">Booth, Marilyn. 2002. [https://books.google.com/books?id=PjK6A8NMuCIC&pg=PR7 "Arab adolescents facing the future"]. pp. 232 in Brown et al., (eds.) ''The World's Youth: Adolescence in Eight Regions of the Globe''. Cambridge: Cambridge University Press. {{ISBN|052180910X}}.</ref>

===Prevalence data===
Examining prevalence data and the characteristics of [[Minor (law)|juvenile]] [[sex offender]]s is a fundamental component to obtain a precise understanding of this heterogeneous group. With mandatory reporting laws in place, it became a necessity for providers to report any incidents of disclosed sexual abuse. Longo and Prescott indicate that juveniles commit approximately 30-60% of all [[child sexual abuse]].<ref name="Longo"/> The Federal Bureau of Investigation Uniform Crime Reports indicate that in 2008 youth under the age of 18 accounted for 16.7% of forcible rapes and 20.61% of other sexual offenses.<ref name="FBI">{{cite web|url=https://www.fbi.gov/ucr/ucr.htm |title=FBI — Uniform Crime Reporting |work=FBI |url-status=dead |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20041024015252/https://www.fbi.gov//ucr/ucr.htm |archive-date=24 October 2004 }}</ref> Center for Sex Offender Management indicates that approximately one-fifth of all [[rape]]s and one-half of all sexual child molestation can be accounted for by juveniles.<ref name="Hunter">{{cite web|last=Hunter|first=J.|date=December 1999|title=The Center for Sex Offender Management. Understanding juvenile sex offending behavior: Emerging research, treatment approaches, and management practices|access-date=11 October 2009|url=http://www.csom.org/pubs/juvbrf10.html|url-status=live|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20091221073119/http://www.csom.org/pubs/juvbrf10.html|archive-date=21 December 2009}}</ref>

===Official record data===
The Office of Juvenile Justice and Delinquency Prevention indicates that 15% of juvenile [[arrest]]s occurred for rape in 2006, and 12% were clearance (resolved by an arrest).<ref name="Snyder">{{cite journal |last=Snyder |first=H. M. |date=November 2008 |journal=Office of Juvenile Justice and Delinquency Prevention |title=Juvenile arrests 2006 |access-date=15 October 2009 |url=http://www.ncjrs.gov/pdffiles1/ojjdp/221338.pdf |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20090508191829/http://www.ncjrs.gov/pdffiles1/ojjdp/221338.pdf |archive-date=8 May 2009 }}</ref> The total number of juvenile arrests in 2006 for forcible rape was 3,610 with 2% being female and 36% being under the age of 15 years.<ref name="Snyder"/> This trend has declined throughout the years with forcible rape from 1997–2006 being −30% and from 2005 to 2006 being −10%.<ref name="Snyder"/> The OJJDP reports that the juvenile arrest rate for forcible rape increased from the early 1980s through the 1990s and at that time it fell again.<ref name="Snyder"/> Violent crime rates in the U.S. have been on a steady decline since the 1990s.<ref>{{Cite journal|last=LaFree|first=Gary|date=August 1999|title=DECLINING VIOLENT CRIME RATES IN THE 1990S: Predicting Crime Booms and Busts|url=http://www.annualreviews.org/doi/10.1146/annurev.soc.25.1.145|journal=Annual Review of Sociology|language=en|volume=25|issue=1|pages=145–168|doi=10.1146/annurev.soc.25.1.145|issn=0360-0572|url-access=subscription}}</ref> The OJJDP also reported that the total number of juvenile arrests in 2006 for sex offenses (other than forcible rape) was 15,900 with 10% being female and 47% being under the age of 15.<ref name="Snyder"/> There was again a decrease with the trend throughout the years with sex offenses from 1997 to 2006 being −16% and from 2005 to 2006 being −9%.<ref name="Snyder"/>

===Males who commit sexual crimes===
Barbaree and Marshall indicate that juvenile males contribute to the majority of sex crimes, with 2–4% of adolescent males having reported committing sexually assaultive behavior, and 20% of all rapes and 30–50% of all [[child molestation]] are perpetrated by adolescent males.<ref name="Barbaree"/> It is clear that males are over-represented in this population. This is consistent with Ryan and Lane's research indicating that males account for 91-93% of the reported juvenile sex offenses.<ref name="Ryan"/> Righthand and Welch reported that females account for an estimated 2–11% of incidents of sexual offending.<ref name="Righthand">{{cite journal |last1=Righthand |first1=S. |last2=Welch |first2=C. |year=2004 |title=Characteristics of youth who sexually offend |journal=Journal of Child Sexual Abuse |volume=13 |issue=3 |pages=15–32 |doi=10.1300/J070v13n03_02 |pmid=15914388 |s2cid=23452835 }}</ref> In addition, it reported by The Office of Juvenile Justice and Delinquency Prevention that in the juvenile arrests during 2006, African American male youth were disproportionately arrested (34%) for forcible rape. In one case in a foster home a 13-year-old boy raped a 9-year-old boy by having forced anal sex with him. In a court hearing the 9-year-old boy said he had done this multiple times. The 13-year-old boy was charged for sexual assault.<ref name="Snyder"/>

===Juvenile sex crimes internationally===
Sexual crimes committed by juveniles are not just an issue in the United States. Studies from the Netherlands show that out of 3,200 sex offenders recorded by police in 2009, 672 of those were juveniles, approximately 21 percent of sexual offenders. The study also points out the male to female ratio of sexual predators.<ref>{{Cite journal |doi = 10.1177/1477370813479077|title = Juvenile female sex offenders: Offender and offence characteristics|journal = European Journal of Criminology|volume = 11|pages = 23–38|year = 2014|last1 = Wijkman|first1 = Miriam|last2 = Bijleveld|first2 = Catrien|last3 = Hendriks|first3 = Jan|s2cid = 145779900}}</ref>

In 2009, a U.S. congressman proposed legislation that would create an International Sex Offender Registry. The bill was introduced due to the fact that because laws differ in different countries, someone who is on the [[Sex offender registries in the United States|sex offender registry in the U.S.]] who may be barred from living certain places and doing certain activities has free range in other less developed countries. This can lead to child sex tourism, when a [[sexual predator]] will go to less developed countries and prey on young boys and girls. Karne Newburn in his article, The Prospect of an International Sex Offender Registry, pointed out some serious flaws in the proposed bill, such as creating safety issues within the communities for the [[sex offender]]s placed on the registry.  Newburn suggested instead of creating an International Sex Offender Registry from the U.S. model the U.S. join other countries in a dialogue on creating an effective model. As of now no registry exists. Despite this there is still interest in creating some sort of international registry.<ref>{{cite web |url=http://hosted.law.wisc.edu/wordpress/wilj/files/2011/10/Newburn_Final_8.2.11-ISSUE-28-3.pdf |title=Wisconsin International Law Journal |access-date=7 August 2016 |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20160303225055/http://hosted.law.wisc.edu/wordpress/wilj/files/2011/10/Newburn_Final_8.2.11-ISSUE-28-3.pdf |archive-date=3 March 2016 }}</ref>

==By country==
===United Kingdom===
The [[United Kingdom]] has three separate and distinct [[criminal justice]] systems: [[England]] and [[Wales]], [[Northern Ireland]], and [[Scotland]]. Young offenders are often dealt with by the [[Youth Offending Team]]. There is concern young adult offenders are not getting the support they need to help them avoid reoffending.<ref>[https://www.bbc.co.uk/news/uk-37770576 Offenders aged 18-25 need more attention to deter crime, say MPs]. ''[[BBC]]''.</ref>

In England and Wales the [[age of criminal responsibility]] is set at 10. Young offenders aged 10 to 17 (i.e. up to their 18th birthday) are classed as a [[juvenile (law)|juvenile]] offender. Between the ages of 18 and 20 (i.e. up to their 21st birthday) they are classed as young offenders. Offenders aged 21 and over are known as adult offenders. 

In Scotland the age of criminal responsibility was formerly set at 8, one of the lowest ages of criminal responsibility in [[Europe]]. It has since been raised to 12 by the Criminal Justice and Licensing (Scotland) Act 2010, which received Royal Assent on 6 August 2010.<ref>{{cite web|url=http://www.scotland.gov.uk/News/Releases/2009/03/27140804|title=Criminal age to be raised to 12|work=scotland.gov.uk|date=March 2009 |access-date=8 February 2015}}</ref><ref>{{cite web|url=http://www.scotland.gov.uk/Topics/Justice/criminal-justice-bill|title=Criminal Justice and Licensing (Scotland) Act 2010|work=scotland.gov.uk|access-date=8 February 2015}}</ref>

In Northern Ireland, the [[age of criminal responsibility]] is 10.

In 2008, police rolled out [[Operation Staysafe]], which allowed police to hand over juvenile delinquents to a [[social worker]].

=== Canada ===
In Canada, the YCJA protects the rights of young offenders. It has four main goals to ensure the youth is subject to meaningful consequences that promote the long-term protection of society, to rehabilitate and reintegrate the youth into society seamlessly, and to prevent crime by examining the underlying causes. The YCJA was introduced in 2003, succeeding the Young Offender's Act.

===Northern Europe===
In both [[Denmark]] and [[Sweden]], the age of criminal responsibility is set at 15. In Sweden it has been 15 since 1902.<ref>{{cite web|url=https://lup.lub.lu.se/student-papers/search/publication/5431512|title=Gammal nog att begå brott, för ung för att straffas- En undersökning om huruvida straffbarhetsåldern är lämplig och ändamålsenligt satt|publisher=Lund University|language=en, sv|author=Madeleine Nählstedt|date=2015|access-date=31 July 2016}}</ref>

=== United States ===
{{mainarticle|Juvenile delinquency in the United States}}
In the [[United States]], the age of criminal responsibility for [[Federal crime in the United States|federal crimes]] is set at 11. While this has been set at the [[Federal government of the United States|federal]] level, each state is responsible for setting their own age of criminal responsibility. 31 states have no minimum age for criminal responsibility, while the remaining 19 do. [[North Carolina]] has the lowest responsibility age of 6 years old and [[Massachusetts]] has the highest of 12 years old.<ref>{{cite web |title=Jurisdictional boundaries |url=http://www.jjgps.org/jurisdictional-boundaries |website=JJGPS |access-date=14 January 2024}}</ref>

There are 1.5 million cases per year in the US that handle [[Status offense|status offenses]] or [[Criminal offense|criminal offenses]] by young offenders. However, only 52 juveniles were fully sentenced to prison-time between 2010–2015. [[Recidivism]] is common among young offenders, with 67% becoming repeat offenders.<ref>{{Cite book|title=Youthful Offenders in the Federal System|last=Cohen|first=Kenneth|publisher=United States Sentencing Commission|year=2017|pages=8}}</ref>

=== Brazil ===
In Brazil, the age of criminal responsibility is set at the age of 18. Anyone that is found guilty of committing crimes prior to the age of 18 is treated to other options rather than jail. These include, for children under 12, foster care options in order to get them a safer family, and, for young offenders over 12, being sentenced to complying with a range of socio-educative measures that can go from a warning to community work and even to internment in specialized facilities, which include basic schooling and occupational training courses that aim at preventing the offenders from resorting to crime to support themselves, although conditions in such facilities are often subpar.<ref>{{Cite web|url=http://www.planalto.gov.br/ccivil_03/leis/l8069.htm|title=Estatuto da Criança e do Adolescente|website=planalto.gov.br|access-date=31 January 2020}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www1.folha.uol.com.br/cotidiano/2019/07/doze-estados-tem-sistema-socioeducativo-lotado.shtml|title=Onze estados têm sistema socioeducativo lotado|date=8 July 2019|website=Folha de S.Paulo|language=pt-BR|access-date=31 January 2020}}</ref> With a spike in crime rates among young offenders occurring in 2015, along with an almost 40% increase in internments of young offenders, there was a push to lower the age of criminal responsibility to 16, which ultimately failed.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://latindispatch.com/2015/07/02/brazilian-congress-rejects-controversial-bill-regarding-age-of-criminal-responsibility/|title=Brazilian Congress Rejects Controversial Bill Regarding Age of Criminal Responsibility|date=2 July 2015|website=Latin America News Dispatch|language=en-US|access-date=31 January 2020}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.bbc.com/news/world-latin-america-32779197|title=The man standing up for young offenders in Brazil|last=Bowater|first=Donna|date=21 May 2015|website=BBC News|access-date=5 December 2018}}</ref>

=== China ===
Juvenile crime has risen in China with an average increase of 5% per year.
